message.content:
Hmm, can you propose altenratives to 'enthrone' and 'commission'?  Here, we don't need perfect names, but we do need unambiguous ones that we won't want to use later.  Any verbs related to installing windows lol?

re: powershell: I think the orchestrating 'finish windows setup' tabtarget is a series of remote powershell actions, each standlone.  Powershell is excruciatingly hard to get clear error status so we'll never use compound powershell.  Thoughts?

content.text:
## More verb alternatives

Most ceremonial verbs are taken in RB (mantle, invest, ordain, abjure, summon, conjure, vouch, plumb, levy, inscribe, enshrine), and others are taken in JJK (enroll, plant). What's left:

**Window-installation flavor (your suggestion, embraced):**

| Verb | Notes |
|---|---|
| **glaze** | Literally "install/finish windows." Past tense: glazed. "Node is glazed." Pairs visually with garrison (garrison fortifies, glaze finishes). Real verb, low reclaim risk, leans into the pun. |
| **fenestrate** | "Provide with windows" — technical/obscure word for window-installation. "Node is fenestrated." Maximally obscure, very low reclaim risk, hardest pun lean. |

**Plain semantic fits:**

| Verb | Notes |
|---|---|
| **lodge** | "Embed permanently in place" — has the sense of formal registration ("lodge a complaint"). "Admin trust is lodged." Low reclaim risk. |
| **dub** | "Confer status by ritual." Short and distinctive. "Node is dubbed." Low reclaim risk but feels playful. |
| **bestow** | "Grant as a gift." But bestow is generic enough you might want it later for permission/privilege concepts — moderate reclaim risk. |

**Less promising but worth listing**:

- **moor** — secure in place. Generic-ish.
- **shore** — provide support. Has "shore up" idiom that confuses.
- **patent** — letters-patent grant of authority. Obscure but noun-heavy.

If you want the pun, **glaze** is the strongest. If you want maximum unambiguity-for-future-safety, **fenestrate** wins (so obscure no one will ever want it for anything else). If you want a serious verb without pun risk, **lodge** is the cleanest.

My order of preference for your stated criteria (unambiguous + won't reclaim later): **fenestrate → glaze → lodge**.

## On standalone-PowerShell-only architecture

Strong yes. Three reasons:

1. **You're not inventing this — it's already project doctrine.** From `Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/jjk-claude-context.md`'s standing notes: *"PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call."* You're applying an established discipline to a new orchestrator. No new pattern to defend, just a new application site.

2. **The PowerShell error model genuinely is broken.** `$?`, `$LASTEXITCODE`, `-ErrorAction Stop`, terminating-vs-non-terminating exceptions, exception types per cmdlet — all subtle and inconsistent. Treating PowerShell as one-atomic-syscall-per-ssh-exec sidesteps all of it. Bash gets the exit code, bash makes the next decision. PowerShell never has to think.

3. **Each step is a unit of recovery.** When step 3 fails, the operator (or the orchestrator) knows exactly which atomic op failed and can re-run from there. Compound PowerShell with try/catch loses this — you can't easily resume halfway through a multi-statement script.

### Implications worth noting

- **Orchestrator is bash on the operator station.** This is remote-run — orchestrator ssh's into admin and sends one PowerShell command per ssh exec. (Earlier I read your "in one of the admin powershells" as local-run; the new message clarifies remote-run with PowerShell as the wire payload. So the handbook's PasswordAuthentication-yes step stays load-bearing — orchestrator needs an auth method to land in admin's session.)

- **Each PowerShell action must be expressible as a single atomic command.** That's mostly fine: `Add-Content`, `icacls`, `Restart-Service`, `Set-Content` are all one-shot. The sshd_config edit is the one to watch — `(Get-Content $path) -replace ... | Set-Content $path` is technically a pipeline but is treated as one PowerShell statement and produces one exit code. That's atomic enough; what you want to avoid is multi-statement scripts with `;` or newlines between independent operations.

- **The Windows file-encoding gotcha lives in the sshd_config-edit atomic op.** `Set-Content` defaults to UTF-16-LE on older PowerShell. Need `Set-Content -Encoding utf8NoBOM` (PowerShell 7+) or the older `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($path, $content, [System.Text.UTF8Encoding]::new($false))` to avoid BOM-induced sshd parse failures. That's a known atomic-op detail to spec correctly, not an architectural concern.

- **Verification of each atomic op is bash's job** (post-condition checks via subsequent ssh exec). E.g., after the sshd_config edit, send another ssh exec with `Select-String -Path C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config -Pattern '^PasswordAuthentication no$'` and check that exit code. Per-step verification gates fall out naturally from this architecture.

The architecture also makes the Posture A/B distinction we hashed out earlier clean: in this orchestrator, each "write sshd_config" atomic op is followed by a "read sshd_config and verify" atomic op. Posture A (writing) and Posture B (verifying) coexist as adjacent steps rather than competing designs.

message.content: All right, 'fenestrate' is exactly the right word for 'finish windows setup including last configuration steps'.  Could we still keep its nature to support 'fenestrating' a different user?  Or should we defer that feature till we have it?  What questions before we either carry out the improvements or slate a pace to do the improvements?

content.text:
Two answers, then the question inventory.

## On fenestrating a different user

**Already supported by the design**, no extra work needed:

- Fenestrate operates on whatever `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` the chosen investiture names. Different investitures pointing at the same node = different admin users. So multi-admin-per-node falls out of the BURP regime data model without any per-call parameter override.

- If fenestrate's pubkey placement stays **additive** (idempotently adds the operator's station pubkey, never removes anyone else's), then multiple operators independently fenestrating against the same admin user on the same node naturally accumulate trust — operator A fenestrates → A's pubkey lands; operator B fenestrates → B's pubkey lands alongside A's. No coordination needed.

- The deeper case — operator B leverages operator A's pre-existing trust to install B's own admin pubkey without B knowing the admin password — is paddock's already-deferred **Adopt** concept. Nothing fenestrate needs to do today to keep adopt as a future option; it's a separate verb when it arrives.

So: fenestrate as designed (operates on BURP-named admin user, additive pubkey placement) already supports the realistic "different user" cases. Defer adopt as planned.

## Open questions before edit-or-slate

These are real design forks I'd want clarity on before either editing files or you slating paces. None of them are pace-structure questions — just design holes I see:

1. **What does fenestrate do for c (Cygwin) and w (WSL) garrisons?** Garrison-c reaches Cygwin sshd; garrison-w reaches WSL sshd; neither is Windows OpenSSH. Garrison's Gate 3 (require `PasswordAuthentication no`) reads which sshd_config — Windows OpenSSH's, or Cygwin's, or WSL's? Three sub-questions:
   - Is Windows OpenSSH the only entry point, with c/w garrisons jumping through it to reach Cygwin/WSL? (Then fenestrate hardens just Windows OpenSSH and we're done.)
   - Or do c/w garrisons reach Cygwin/WSL sshd directly on different ports? (Then those sshds also need hardening — fenestrate-c, fenestrate-w, or operator-manual?)
   - Or is Windows OpenSSH solely a setup-time scaffold that's irrelevant once Cygwin/WSL is reachable? (Then maybe Windows OpenSSH gets uninstalled at some point, and fenestrate's sshd_config concern is moot.)

2. **Linux/Mac — fenestrate or operator-manual?** Symmetric universe (fenestrate on every platform) is uniform but adds a verb where ssh-copy-id+sed already covers it. Asymmetric (fenestrate is Windows-only) is honest about Windows being the fiddly platform but means the BUSJ* spec catalog has to explain why. Which?

3. **Tabtarget colophon for fenestrate.** Per option (c), it's uniform — no shell-letter suffix. Candidates:
   - `buw-jf` — jurisdiction-fenestrate. Shortest, parallels `buw-jw{k,c,s}` shape.
   - `buw-jpf` — jurisdiction-privileged-fenestrate. Parallels current `buw-jpg{b,c,w}` (jurisdiction-privileged-garrison) naming.
   - Or some other shape you prefer.

4. **Concept identifier.** `busn_fenestrate` parallel to `busn_garrison`? Or some other prefix to mark these are operations vs. tabtarget vesture?

5. **Module placement.** Does fenestrate's implementation live in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` alongside garrison helpers, or get its own seat (`bujf_fenestrate.sh` or similar)? `bujb` was minted as the jurisdiction implementation seat; broadening it to host both verbs is reasonable, splitting it gives sharper module boundaries.

6. **Drift cleanup scope under the restructure.** Earlier we identified four spec-drift sites from the dual-purpose pivot:
   - BUS0:1638 (`busn_garrison` canonical 7-step ceremony)
   - BUS0:1107-1109 (`burp_privileged_key_file` "no password fallback")
   - BUS0:1462-1463 (§Remote Node Access "out-of-band handbook procedure")
   - BUSJGC:20 + BUSJGW:25 preconditions
   
   Under the fenestrate/garrison split, these all need restructuring (not just amending) — `busn_garrison` shrinks back to workload-only; `busn_fenestrate` is born and absorbs the admin-trust steps; `burp_privileged_key_file` body is rewritten around fenestrate; §Remote Node Access intro changes. **Does the fenestrate/garrison split work include all this BUS0 restructuring, or do you want it scoped narrower?**

7. **Handbook step 4 — keep or drop?** Current handbook (commit dc505472) tells operator to set `PasswordAuthentication yes` in sshd_config because we expected garrison to need password fallback for first run. Under fenestrate-does-the-write design, fenestrate ALSO needs an auth method to first land in admin's session — so the handbook step still earns its keep. But fenestrate's first action could be: open ssh with password fallback → immediately set PasswordAuthentication no in sshd_config → restart sshd → from then on key auth only. So PasswordAuthentication=yes is still needed BEFORE fenestrate runs. Step stays. But: should fenestrate also flip it to `no` itself (Posture A on fenestrate, which we agreed) — confirming.

8. **Implementation order.** When the work happens, what builds before what? Fenestrate spec → fenestrate implementation → garrison spec restructure → garrison Gate 2/3 implementation → drift cleanup → handbook update? Or different order? (This is genuinely an implementation-order question, not pace-structure — happy to defer to your judgment.)

Once these are answered I either know what to edit or know enough that you can write a precise pace docket.

content.text:
Confirming each answer, with one pushback.

1. ✓ Fenestrate is Windows-OpenSSH-only. Doesn't touch Cygwin/WSL sshd at all — those are garrison's territory, kept entirely separate.

2. ✓ Linux/Mac stays operator-manual: handbook line says "use `ssh-copy-id`," no tabtarget.

3. ✓ Colophon `buw-jpF`. Capital-letter convention for persistent config application matches existing project pattern (`rbw-aM`, `rbw-cC`, `rbw-dL`, etc.). I'll spec frontispiece as `Fenestrate` with imprint channel `param1` (BURP investiture).

4. ✓ Concept identifier `busn_fenestrate`, parallel to `busn_garrison`.

5. ✓ Implementation seat stays in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`.

6. ✓ Drift cleanup is in scope: BUS0:1638 (busn_garrison restructure to workload-only), BUS0:1107-1109 (burp_privileged_key_file body), BUS0:1462-1463 (§Remote Node Access intro), BUSJGC:20 + BUSJGW:25 preconditions, plus the §Garrison-Destructive Model paragraph that currently says "garrison subsumes lifecycle" needs refactoring around the split.

## 7 — pushback needed

We cannot drop the handbook step yet, **because of an unresolved earlier question I think we glossed past**: is fenestrate local-run or remote-run?

You wrote earlier: *"the orchestrating 'finish windows setup' tabtarget is a series of remote powershell actions, each standlone."* I read that as **remote-run** (orchestrator on operator station; ssh's into rocket; sends one PowerShell command per ssh exec). Under that reading:

- Fenestrate's first ssh attempt needs an auth method to land
- The only auth method available before fenestrate has done its work is password
- Password requires `PasswordAuthentication yes` in sshd_config
- Therefore the operator must enable PasswordAuthentication=yes BEFORE fenestrate runs
- Fenestrate then flips it back to no as part of its own work
- **Handbook step 4 stays** (it sets up the auth method fenestrate needs to first land)

Under the alternative reading — **local-run** (operator invokes fenestrate's PowerShell directly on the Windows host in their elevated PowerShell session, no SSH involved at all) — then yes, handbook step 4 can be dropped because fenestrate edits sshd_config locally without ever needing SSH password auth. The PasswordAuthentication directive stays at its shipped default until fenestrate sets it to `no`.

Local-run has architecture costs we discussed earlier:
- Doesn't fit BUK's bash-tabtarget pattern naturally — would be a PowerShell script file the operator runs by hand on the Windows host, OR a bash tabtarget that prints PowerShell content for the operator to copy-paste-and-run, OR a new species of tabtarget BUK has to learn
- No remote orchestrator, so all logic is in PowerShell (the very thing we wanted to keep small via the atomic-syscall discipline)

Remote-run keeps the architecture clean (bash orchestrator + atomic PowerShell ops over ssh) at the cost of one persistent handbook step.

**Which?** If remote-run, handbook step 4 stays. If local-run, it goes. That's the only thing I need answered before everything's decided.

Yes, the handbook must specify how to configure windows machine locally for temporary password use, but then remote run 'fenestrate' resolves it by disabling password access and restarting service programatically.  Is that a complete answer?  Whoops!  Can that even work, restart sshd within an sshd session?

content.text:
Yes, that's a complete answer in design intent — and you're right to flag the restart issue, it's a real wrinkle.

## Can you restart sshd from within an sshd session?

**On Windows OpenSSH: probably not gracefully.** Unlike Linux (where per-session sshd children survive a `systemctl restart sshd` because they're forked off), Windows' service model is monolithic — `Restart-Service sshd` stops the service which kills its child sessions. Your in-flight SSH session dies.

But this is **manageable, not blocking**, because the bash orchestrator can expect the disconnect and treat it as a phase boundary. The pattern:

### Two-phase fenestrate

**Phase 1 — password session (operator pre-enabled `PasswordAuthentication yes` per handbook)**
1. ssh exec: place admin pubkey in `administrators_authorized_keys` (atomic op)
2. ssh exec: `icacls` lock-down on the file (atomic op)
3. ssh exec: rewrite sshd_config with `PasswordAuthentication no` etc., utf8NoBOM (atomic op)
4. ssh exec: `sshd -t -f C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config` **validate the config before touching the running service** (atomic op — *load-bearing safety: if this fails, abort without restarting; otherwise a bad config bricks sshd and locks the operator out*)
5. ssh exec: `Restart-Service sshd` (atomic op — **expect this to disconnect**; bash orchestrator treats SSH disconnect immediately after this exec as the expected end of phase 1, not a failure)

**Phase 2 — key session (auth method changed; opens fresh ssh with `IdentitiesOnly=yes -i <admin-privkey>`)**
6. ssh exec: re-read sshd_config, verify `PasswordAuthentication no` is present (atomic op — Posture-A's post-write verification)
7. ssh exec: optional probe — confirm key auth actually works in this fresh session (it does, since we got here via key auth)

If Phase 2 connection fails: fenestrate failed somewhere; operator fixes (RDP/console in to inspect and repair sshd_config) and re-runs.

### Why this works

- **The disconnect after `Restart-Service` isn't an error**, it's the expected handoff between auth phases. Bash orchestrator just needs to know "exec #5 may return a non-zero ssh exit code; don't treat that as failure."
- **`sshd -t` validation is the lockout-safety gate.** It checks the new config without applying it. If sshd_config has a typo or a Windows-rejected directive (we hit one of these earlier — UsePAM doesn't exist on Windows), the config is bad and `sshd -t` catches it. Fenestrate aborts without restarting; sshd keeps running on the previous (working) config; operator fixes via RDP and re-runs.
- **Phase 2 connection itself is the proof** — if key auth opens fresh, hardening succeeded; verification atomic ops are confirming details.
- **Industry-standard pattern.** Ansible, Chef, Salt all do bootstrap sequences that flip auth methods mid-run by design — they handle the disconnect by reconnecting under the new auth.

### What this means for the design

- Handbook still has the "Enable PasswordAuthentication yes" step (load-bearing — phase 1 needs it).
- Fenestrate has implicit two-phase structure: password-session + key-session, with `Restart-Service sshd` as the boundary.
- Bash orchestrator owns the phase transition (knows to reopen ssh after the restart exec).
- Pre-restart validation (`sshd -t`) is required, not optional — it's the difference between "fenestrate aborts with diagnostic" and "operator is locked out and has to console in."

So yes, the design is complete. The Restart-Service-from-within-its-own-session wrinkle costs you ~3 lines of orchestrator logic (treat post-restart disconnect as expected; reopen with key auth) and one extra atomic op (`sshd -t` before the restart). Both are improvements, not blockers.

message.content: This was quite a lot of discussion and change.  I'm now musing we should slate 1 or 2 paces to carry out this improvement.  Your thoughts?  They will go first in heat A-.

content.text:
## My read

The work falls naturally into two clusters by character:

**Cluster 1 — Concept introduction + design landing.** Mint `busn_fenestrate` in BUS0; reshape `busn_garrison` to workload-only with the canonical 7-step shape; restructure BUSJGB/C/W to drop the admin-trust front-half and add the two verification gates; add BUSJPF subdoc with fenestrate's two-phase contract (password phase steps 1-5, key phase steps 6-7, including `sshd -t` validation gate); update mapping section + tabtarget catalog (13 → 14); update paddock §Current Concept and §Garrison ceremony / introduce §Fenestrate ceremony.

**Cluster 2 — Drift cleanup.** BUS0:1107-1109 (`burp_privileged_key_file` body — "no password fallback" prose); BUS0:1462-1463 (§Remote Node Access intro — "out-of-band handbook procedure" prose); §Garrison-Destructive Model paragraph reframing (acknowledge fenestrate carved off the admin-trust portion). Plus updating `buhj_jurisdiction.sh` to mention fenestrate in the post-bootstrap hand-off (currently says "Run Garrison" only — should say "Run Fenestrate, then Garrison").

## Honest reasoning on 1 vs 2

**Argument for 1 pace**: The clusters are tightly coupled. The drift sites in BUS0 are stale precisely because fenestrate now exists to absorb the work they describe; fixing them in the same notch as fenestrate's introduction produces one coherent state transition. The handbook update is small. A single mechanical-execution pace lands the whole design as one consistent commit, easier to bisect later than a two-step landing.

**Argument for 2 paces**: Cluster 1 is dense design work (mint a concept, restructure another concept, draft a new operation spec, update mapping + catalog, paddock surgery). Cluster 2 is mostly prose sweeping (find-and-fix three to four spec passages, update handbook hand-off prose, paddock standing note adjustment). They have different cognitive characters — one is "hold the new design in your head and land it"; the other is "grep for stale phrases and fix each in turn." Splitting matches that, and gives a clean stopping point if the design pace runs long.

## My lean

**Single pace, with the caveat that you size the docket honestly.** The clusters are too tightly coupled in time and content to benefit from a sequential split — Cluster 2's prose changes only land correctly if you have the new design fully in mind, and pace 2 would inherit pace 1's full context anyway. Splitting also creates a brief window where the spec is internally inconsistent (fenestrate exists in catalog but BUS0 §Remote Node Access intro still says admin trust is out-of-band).

Counter-pressure: if you suspect the cluster-1 docket will run long enough to feel "two-paces-worth-of-work," the split has real value. The cluster-2 work is fully shovel-ready (no design judgment, just prose alignment), so it could sit as its own pace cleanly. Sometimes "one pace by content" produces a docket that reads sprawling; splitting then earns its keep purely as a planning aid.

If you're confident the docket can be tight (say, 12-15 lines specifying scope + done-when + out-of-scope), single pace. If the docket starts wanting subsections, split.

That's the honest tradeoff. I'd lean single, but the call is squarely yours.

agreeing on a new verb decomposition. Spec-internal consistency is the

uniform (no shell-letter), tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent
config application; takes BURP investiture as param1), implementation
seat shared with garrison in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Locked design:
two-phase remote-run ceremony — password-session followed by
key-session; `sshd -t` validation as load-bearing gate before any sshd
restart (lockout safety). Posture A — fenestrate writes sshd_config,
that is its job. Add BUSJPF subdoc carrying the two-phase operation
contract; update mapping section + tabtarget catalog (13 → 14).

Narrow `busn_garrison` to workload provisioning only — the admin-trust
front-half moves to fenestrate. Update BUSJGB/C/W to drop the
front-half and add two verification gates: ssh express-test of key
auth before destructive workload work (lockout prevention);
file-content check requiring `PasswordAuthentication no` with
operator-guidance failure if absent (Posture B — garrison verifies,
never writes sshd_config). Garrison stays per-shell-letter b/c/w;
fenestrate is uniform.

Sweep BUS0 drift sites that the dual-purpose pivot left and that
fenestrate now honestly covers. Discovery recipe:
`grep -rn "out-of-band\|no password fallback\|operator-bootstrapped" Tools/buk/`.
Reframe §Garrison-Destructive Model paragraph (fenestrate carved off
admin trust; garrison still subsumes workload lifecycle with no
separate materialize/remove/enumerate verbs).

Update `buhj_jurisdiction.sh` post-bootstrap hand-off prose to "Run
Fenestrate, then Garrison." Linux/Mac stays operator-manual via
ssh-copy-id handbook line (no fenestrate equivalent). Update paddock
§Current Concept (introduce fenestrate alongside garrison), split
ceremony section into §Fenestrate ceremony + §Garrison ceremony,
adjust standing notes that no longer match.

Done when:
- BUS0 reflects the split with no contradicting prose anywhere
- BUSJPF + revised BUSJGB/C/W carry their respective contracts
- Handbook and paddock no longer claim garrison handles admin trust
- `bujb_jurisdiction` body in BUS0 acknowledges the verb shared seat

Out of scope: any code (implementation is `implement-jurisdiction`'s
job); PowerShell garrison + dispatch (deferred per paddock); adopt
verb (deferred); end-to-end operator test on a Windows node (waits on
implementation existing).

message.content: please now review the docket.  Make a list of suggestions for eliminating errors, ambiguities, and/or assuring correctness.

content.text:
Going through it critically. Found one real error and several ambiguities.

## Errors (correctness problems)

**1. Garrison c/w verification gate targets the wrong sshd.** The docket says BUSJGB/C/W all get the same Posture-B gate ("file-content check requiring `PasswordAuthentication no`"). But:
- garrison-**b** reaches Linux/Mac sshd → checks `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` on the target node
- garrison-**c** reaches Cygwin sshd → checks Cygwin's `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` inside Cygwin userland
- garrison-**w** reaches WSL sshd → checks WSL distro's `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` inside WSL

**None of those is what fenestrate hardens.** Fenestrate hardens Windows OpenSSH's `C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config`, which is a *fourth* sshd irrelevant to garrison-c/w's auth path. So the docket's implication that "fenestrate prepares the state garrison's gate verifies" is wrong for c/w, and possibly for b too (Linux/Mac has no fenestrate at all per design — operator does ssh-copy-id manually).

This needs to be reconciled. Either:
- The docket explicitly says: garrison's gate verifies the sshd it actually connects to (operator-manually-hardened in all cases), and fenestrate's role is solely to harden Windows OpenSSH for whoever uses it directly; OR
- Fenestrate's scope expands beyond Windows OpenSSH; OR
- Some other reframing the user wants.

As written, a mount agent would plausibly implement the gate against the wrong file paths.

**2. Colophon `buw-jpF` convention conflicts with existing `buw-jpg[bcw]`.** The capital-F = persistent-config reasoning doesn't survive against the existing garrison family — `g` (garrison) is lowercase yet garrison is destructive/persistent. Three resolutions, all coherent, none chosen:
- Rename `buw-jpg` → `buw-jpG` (garrison was mis-cased; fenestrate as `buw-jpF` retroactively justifies the convention)
- Use `buw-jpf` (lowercase, matching the existing family's case)
- Accept inconsistency and document why fenestrate uses capital while garrison uses lowercase

The docket locks `buw-jpF` without addressing this. Mount agent may either propagate the inconsistency or flag it back.

## Ambiguities (mount agent might decide differently than intended)

**3. Phase boundary rationale missing.** The docket says "two-phase remote-run ceremony — password-session followed by key-session" but doesn't say WHY two phases. The load-bearing reason is that `Restart-Service sshd` kills the password session — that's what forces the boundary. Without this, a mount agent might design a single-session ceremony that toggles PasswordAuthentication at the end without restart, or restart inside the same session and fail mysteriously.

**4. `sshd -t` gate location within the ceremony unspecified.** The docket says it's "before any sshd restart" but doesn't pin down that it must be the immediate predecessor of Restart-Service (so a malformed config can't slip through any intervening op). Mount agent might place it earlier and lose the safety property.

**5. Subdoc filename `BUSJPF` assumed but not user-approved.** I named it in the docket; user only approved `busn_fenestrate` (concept) and `buw-jpF` (tabtarget). Mount agent will follow the pattern but the user might want a different filename (BUSJF? BUSJP?). Worth explicit.

**6. "PasswordAuthentication no" check semantics.** A literal string match against the file is naive — a `#PasswordAuthentication no` (commented) would erroneously match while not actually being effective. The right check is the effective value via `sshd -T` parse output. Docket doesn't specify, leaving the mount agent (or future implementer) to choose.

**7. "bujb_jurisdiction body in BUS0 acknowledges the verb shared seat" is vague.** What specifically needs to land there? The current body lists hardcoded mappings (shell-letter command= directives, workload key paths, WSL distro name). Does fenestrate add anything to that hardcoded list (Windows OpenSSH paths)? Or does fenestrate just share the module file without contributing to the hardcoded data? Unclear.

**8. Paddock §Current Concept update larger than implied.** Docket says "introduce fenestrate alongside garrison" — but the current §Current Concept describes a unified 9-step garrison ceremony. The split means restructuring the whole §Garrison ceremony narrative, not just adding a fenestrate paragraph. Mount agent will figure it out from Done-when, but the docket undersells the scope here.

## Worth noting

**9. Implementation order unspecified.** Whether to land fenestrate first then narrow garrison, or do both in lockstep, isn't stated. Probably fine for a single-pace scope but a mount agent might want to know whether to commit incrementally.

**10. Discovery grep matches may not all need fixing.** The recipe `grep -rn "out-of-band\|no password fallback\|operator-bootstrapped"` will surface drift candidates but also could hit non-load-bearing prose (e.g., the Linux/Mac handbook note legitimately discusses operator-side recipes). Mount agent has to judge each. Worth a sentence in the docket noting this is a candidate list, not a fix list.

**11. "13 → 14" tabtarget count assertion**: durable since this pace lands first in A-, but worth knowing it's a slate-time count that depends on no intervening tabtarget changes.

## Crucial vs cosmetic

The first issue (Error 1) is the only one I'd insist on resolving before mount — it's a real design hole that would lead the mount agent to a wrong implementation. Error 2 is a real naming choice that the user should make consciously. The ambiguities are real but mount-survivable; mount agent will reason through them and probably land in the right place.

If you fix Error 1 and decide on Error 2, the docket is solid. The ambiguities are nice-to-have clarifications.

## Character

Design landing — multiple spec files, handbook, and paddock must end
agreeing on a new verb decomposition. Spec-internal consistency is the
load-bearing property; not a mechanical sweep.

## Docket

Introduce `busn_fenestrate` as a new jurisdiction verb. Targets
Windows OpenSSH only (hardens its `sshd_config`), uniform (no
shell-letter), tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config
application; takes BURP investiture as param1), implementation seat
shared with garrison in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Locked design:

- Two-phase remote-run ceremony forced by the fact that
  `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own ssh session. Phase 1
  ends with Restart-Service as its final atomic op; phase 2
  reconnects via key auth for any post-state verification. Single
  ssh exec for the restart; bash orchestrator treats the disconnect
  immediately after that exec as the expected phase boundary.
- `sshd -t` validation is the penultimate atomic op of phase 1
  (immediately before Restart-Service), so a malformed config
  aborts fenestrate before it could brick the running sshd.
- Posture A — fenestrate writes sshd_config, that is its job.
  Verify-after-write follows the show-raw-then-bash-parse pattern:
  PowerShell `Get-Content` returns raw file bytes; bash-side
  BCG-compliant matching extracts effective directive values. Keeps
  PowerShell out of the parsing business (its error semantics are
  unreliable).
- Manual password is needed only for the phase-1 initial ssh
  session (handbook precondition: `PasswordAuthentication yes`);
  everything after is automated key auth.

Add BUSJPF subdoc carrying the two-phase operation contract; update
mapping section + tabtarget catalog (13 → 14).

Narrow `busn_garrison` to workload provisioning only — admin-trust
front-half moves to fenestrate. Garrison never reads or writes any

OpenSSH only; for c/w garrisons, Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening is
operator-manual; for b garrison, Linux/Mac sshd is operator-manual
likewise.) Garrison's first natural action is admin SSH via key
auth — failure there surfaces immediately. Update BUSJGB/C/W to
reflect the workload-only contract. Garrison stays per-shell-letter;

is also persistent/destructive; the existing lowercase `g` was

fenestrate now honestly covers. Discovery recipe (candidates, not

Reframe §Garrison-Destructive Model paragraph (fenestrate carved off

content.text:
Quick answers, then revised docket.

**1.** You're right — drop Gate 3 (the sshd_config check). Garrison's responsibility is workload only; sshd posture is fenestrate's (Windows OpenSSH) or operator-manual (everything else).

I'd extend the same logic to **Gate 2** (the SSH key-auth express-test). Its original rationale was lockout prevention before garrison's own sshd hardening — and garrison no longer hardens, so the lockout scenario doesn't exist. Without Gate 2, garrison just attempts admin SSH as its first natural action and surfaces failure immediately if key auth doesn't work. Flag if you want Gate 2 kept as defense in depth; I've dropped it in the revision but it's a 5-line restore.

**2.** Not pushing back on `[bcw]`. They stay. Only the case convention was the issue. With `buw-jpg[bcw]` → `buw-jpG[bcw]`, the family is consistent with `buw-jpF`. The rename is spec-only since none of the tabtargets exist on disk yet.

**3.** Just editorial. Will tighten the language: phase 1 ends with `Restart-Service sshd` as its final atomic op (which terminates the session); phase 2 reconnects via key auth. Single ssh exec for the restart, exactly as you described.

**4.** Yes, clear placement: `sshd -t` is the **penultimate atomic op of phase 1** — immediately before `Restart-Service`, so a malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick the running sshd. And yes, the manual password is needed only for the phase-1 initial ssh session; everything after is automated key auth.

Now revising the docket via `jjx_redocket`.

# jjezs_reslate A-AAp

## Character

Design landing — multiple spec files, handbook, and paddock must end
agreeing on a new verb decomposition. Spec-internal consistency is the
load-bearing property; not a mechanical sweep.

## Docket

Introduce `busn_fenestrate` as a new jurisdiction verb. Targets
Windows OpenSSH only (hardens its `sshd_config`), uniform (no
shell-letter), tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config
application; takes BURP investiture as param1), implementation seat
shared with garrison in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Locked design:

- Two-phase remote-run ceremony forced by the fact that
  `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own ssh session. Phase 1
  ends with Restart-Service as its final atomic op; phase 2
  reconnects via key auth for any post-state verification. Single
  ssh exec for the restart; bash orchestrator treats the disconnect
  immediately after that exec as the expected phase boundary.
- `sshd -t` validation is the penultimate atomic op of phase 1
  (immediately before Restart-Service), so a malformed config
  aborts fenestrate before it could brick the running sshd.
- Posture A — fenestrate writes sshd_config, that is its job.
  Verify-after-write follows the show-raw-then-bash-parse pattern:
  PowerShell `Get-Content` returns raw file bytes; bash-side
  BCG-compliant matching extracts effective directive values. Keeps
  PowerShell out of the parsing business (its error semantics are
  unreliable).
- Manual password is needed only for the phase-1 initial ssh
  session (handbook precondition: `PasswordAuthentication yes`);
  everything after is automated key auth.

Add BUSJPF subdoc carrying the two-phase operation contract; update
mapping section + tabtarget catalog (13 → 14).

`grep -rn "out-of-band\|no password fallback\|operator-bootstrapped" Tools/buk/`.
Reframe §Garrison-Destructive Model paragraph (fenestrate carved off
admin trust; garrison still subsumes workload lifecycle with no
separate materialize/remove/enumerate verbs).

Update `buhj_jurisdiction.sh` post-bootstrap hand-off to "Run

content.text:
Reslated ₢A-AAp with all the corrections:

- **Gates 2 and 3 both dropped** (garrison no longer reads or writes any sshd_config; flag if you want Gate 2 back)
- **Phase boundary made concrete**: phase 1 ends with `Restart-Service` as final atomic op, bash treats post-restart disconnect as expected
- **`sshd -t` placement specified**: penultimate atomic op of phase 1, immediately before Restart-Service
- **Verify-after-write pattern specified**: PowerShell `Get-Content` raw → bash BCG-compliant matching
- **`buw-jpg[bcw]` → `buw-jpG[bcw]` rename added** with discovery recipe
- **Garrison/sshd target hole closed**: explicit that fenestrate is Windows-OpenSSH-only; c/w garrisons handle their own sshds (operator-manual)
- **Paddock §Current Concept restructure called out** as not-just-additive
- **Discovery grep clarified** as candidates-not-fixes
- **`bujb_jurisdiction` Done-when simplified** to "acknowledges housing both verbs"
- **Tabtarget catalog count** added to Done-when as 14 entries with the rename applied

Ready for your next review pass on the revised docket — or to wrap ₢A-AAo if we're done with that one first.
